Patch Set 2:

Patrick, it would be good to get a sanityn test for this as you described - one client does a short append to the file, then the second client does a sparse (non-append) write to the next stripe, and the first client does another short append.  Something like:

        $LFS setstripe -c 2 -S 1M $DIR/$tfile
        echo "foo" >> $DIR/$tfile
        dd if=/dev/zero of=$DIR2/$tfile bs=1M count=1 seek=1 ||
               error "sparse dd $DIR2/$tfile failed"
        echo "foo" >> $DIR/$tfile

        local data=$(dd if=$DIR2/$tfile bs=1 count=3 skip=$((2 * 1048576)))
        [ "$data" = "foo" ] || error "append to sparse file failed"

Patch Set 2: Code-Review-1

I think the patch itself looks good, but would like to have a test for this.

Patch Set 4: Code-Review-1

attached test passed for me with and without tiny_write code.
One problem found. lustre 2.10 have release a lock fast, so second process don't wait a seconds to write execute.
as about size and o_append, we need to worry about mixing new and old data, but it's possible if write have more than one page size. One page write have an atomic modifications from my view.

Just current master: Test fails
Current master + This patch: Test passes
Current master with tiny writes removed: Test passes

Maybe there's a problem with the test and it's unreliable?  You do have to have at least two stripes available in your test config.

Patch Set 5:

Alexey pointed out elsewhere that it might be enough to just make sure we do a real glimpse rather than just ll_merge_attr.  I'm trying to decide whether or not I think that's safe.  I think it probably is - If we have a correct size as of *some point during our write syscall*, it's OK to write there (as long as no one else can write there at the same time).

But glimpse is a pretty expensive thing to do if there is no dirty page.  It requires creating an I/O and doing the actual glimpse work.  Hm.

I'm still happy with this fix for right now because of the possible performance cost of having to do that glimpse every time.  But at some point exploring that would be a good idea.

Patch Set 5: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 5: Code-Review-1

(1 comment)

I suspect the original approach was wrong after all and we might want to redo this.

A good first step would be to expose an option to disable this tiny write support completely.

Other problems you are going to hit: ll_tiny_write_begin() tries to check page flags on an unlocked page - this is bound to be racy so if the page is transitioning from dirty to writeback - you might miss it and try to write into a page that's already in transit, with possibly data corrupting consequences.

Then what about truncates in the middle of this page we are updating?

I think it's very important to grab your (ldlm) locks first (this would help with the O_APPEND case as well - something that would be a great benefit to tinywrite patch in cases like syslog yet something that's being killed here).

Please check patches for original bugzilla ticket 14010 for inspiration and talk to shadow and panda (original authors of that patch).

Patch Set 5:

So, I was specifically asked to remove the option to disable it in the original patch.  I'll add that back if you'd prefer.

The page *is* locked when we check the flags - look at ll_write_begin.  The page is locked before entering ll_tiny_write_begin, when we do the grab.
vmpage = grab_cache_page_nowait(mapping, index);
^-- returns a locked page (or nothing).

As for a truncate in the middle of the page, should block because the page is locked, I believe.  Otherwise it should work OK, I think - A lot of what could happen is covered by the fact that the write & the truncate are racing already.

A large part of the point of tiny writes is to not have to check the LDLM state directly.  Doing so would somewhat defeat the point.

Shadow reviewed the original, and we've talked about it extensively.  He also thinks we should fix O_APPEND by getting an updated file size, I agree but haven't had time.  I agree it would be good to get O_APPEND working for log files

Patch Set 5:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 5:

> So, I was specifically asked to remove the option to disable it in
 > the original patch.  I'll add that back if you'd prefer.

Yes, please add it back as either a mount option or a proc setting (probably better), as I am afraid more stuff will come out in this code that we might need to disable it "in the field".

 > The page *is* locked when we check the flags - look at
 > ll_write_begin.  The page is locked before entering
 > ll_tiny_write_begin, when we do the grab.
 > vmpage = grab_cache_page_nowait(mapping, index);
 > ^-- returns a locked page (or nothing).

ok, I missed it was locking a page, which is good.

 > A large part of the point of tiny writes is to not have to check
 > the LDLM state directly.  Doing so would somewhat defeat the point.

The bug 14010 worked that around by going page->lock route directly so the overhead was really minimal, I think. We can probably go page->cl_lock of some sort directly here as well and get all the ldlm locks in one go even (my clio knowledge is somewhat rusty now so you'll need to look into actual details)

I think not pinning the ldlm lock might be risky in other ways otherwise (and then again we'll have the whole O_APPEND situation
that does require us to get the locks).

 > Shadow reviewed the original, and we've talked about it
 > extensively.  He also thinks we should fix O_APPEND by getting an
 > updated file size, I agree but haven't had time.  I agree it would
 > be good to get O_APPEND working for log files

Note you cannot "get updated file size" without actually obtaining a write lock that covers entire file. Don't depend on glimpses, they are inherently racy. Also you cannot do a glimpse to decide which stripe to then lock since that is also racy.

Patch Set 5:

Oleg,

Still planning to make/explore other changes, but was thinking about this for appends last night.

The basic idea of tiny writes is that it finds an already dirty page and knows that the work of creating + tracking it is done, so it can use that page without doing that work.

If tiny writes does not find a page where the write is going, it cannot handle the write and must defer to the normal write path.

For appends, we must know the correct file size to have the offset to write at.  If there are other non-append operations racing with us, it's enough to have *a* correct file size from some time during our write.  But as you pointed out, glimpse is not enough to guarantee that (we could complete a glimpse to one stripe and before we do glimpse to a different stripe an i/o could happen to the stripe we already glimpsed and then to the stripe we're going to glimpse, all before we glimpse that second stripe - So we are not guaranteed to have a file size that was *ever* correct.).

So, we must do LDLM locking to get the correct file size, both so we can do the append write correctly and so we can check for a dirty page.

We also have to take care w/the layout (check out my patch for LU-9341), meaning we need to check the layout, lock it, get file size, then check the layout and verify it hasn't changed (and restart if not).  If it hasn't changed, then we know the file size we got is correct.

So now, after all this work, we can check that there is a dirty page to append to.  And if there is not, then we have to start a normal I/O.

I think this would still offer performance improvements for a logging a few bytes at a time case, but it might hurt performance in other cases, and it's comparatively complicated.

Patch Set 5:

About cl_locks for possibly interacting w/the append case, which now I see is your suggestion (which would help w/the problems I described, at least a little):
cl_locks are no longer cached as they used to be, they do not outlive the i/o.  So I don't think there's anything that connects all of the OSC/LDLM locks associated with the I/O that created our page.  And this still leaves the layout issue to address.

Patch Set 5:

you are right that layout further complicates things for O_APPEND.

But the dirty page -> safe to update logic is also somewhat unsafe, I feel. Like what if your dirty page needs to update file size too? There are probably some such corner cases that we are not seeing yet but I feel like might emerge.

What about mirror writes (I guess since we don;t implement this, it would be the headache for whoever implements it later)?

Al in all I feel like a global "turn tiny writes" switch in debugfs is a prudent move.

Patch Set 5:

Oh, I agree *completely* about the switch.  I was only disagreeing (a little) about the LDLM stuff.

I will implement the switch and the other change you suggested, sorry for the delay.

Patch Set 7:

Btw, Oleg, I don't have time to do it right now, but we could maybe get enough benefit for log files to make this worth our while:
If we limited it to singly striped files, we could mark the file (maybe the inode) saying the last write was an append that would leave a dirty page.  That way, as long as we handled that 
flag and other I/Os correctly, we could use that to decide to attempt an append tiny write - Because if we know we have a singly striped file and we know a dirty page is from an append, then we know the file is locked safely if the page is still there.

There are a few wrinkles to writing that, but done correctly I think we could get most of the benefit of tiny writes for appending w/singly striped files without doing anything crazy.  (It's important that checking whether or not to do a tiny write is cheap and simple.)

I guess I'll open an LU to track that, and try to do it if I get time in the future.  It might be too situational to be of value...

Patch Set 7:

Singly striped file w/out a composite layout.
Patch Set 7: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 7:

single striped files are also somewhat tricky.

You need to have not just the dirty page (how do you arrive at a dirty page without knowing file size, btw? and in order to have good file size you must have the lock first)

parents.subject: LU-10520 mkfs: enable extents for big MDT

commit.subject: LU-10681: Disable tiny writes for append
commit.message:
LU-10681: Disable tiny writes for append

Unfortunately, tiny writes do not work correctly with
appending to files.  When appending to a file, we must take
DLM locks to EOF on all stripes, in order to protect file
size so we can append correctly.

If we dirty a page with a normal write then append to it
with a tiny write, these DLM locks are not present, and we
can use an incorrect size if another client writes to a
different stripe, increasing the size without cancelling
the lock which is protecting our dirty page.

We could theoretically check to make sure the required DLM
locks are held, but this would be time consuming.

The simplest solution is to just not allow tiny writes when
appending.

Also add option to disable tiny writes at runtime.

Cray-bug-id: LUS-5723

Change-Id: Ic9421faa3d0268d907040881e8ba3c894261fd49
Signed-off-by: Patrick Farrell <paf@cray.com>
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/31353
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Bobi Jam <bobijam@hotmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
